---
title: "Where Every Drop Goes"
subtitle: "Hidden Maps · Episode 5 — The James River Watershed as a Network"
author: "Troy Altus"
date: "June 2026"
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "On this page"
    number-sections: true
    self-contained: true
    fig-align: center
    include-in-header: toc.css
    include-after-body: confluence-link.js
    grid:
      margin-width: 320px
      body-width: 760px
      gutter-width: 1.5rem
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    classoption: [letterpaper, 11pt]
    geometry:
      - top=1in
      - bottom=1in
      - left=1.25in
      - right=1in
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    toc: true
    pdf-engine: latexmk
    pdf-engine-opts:
      - -lualatex
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  daemon: false
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

import json
import pickle
from pathlib import Path

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import networkx as nx
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

KM_TO_MI = 0.621371
SQKM_TO_SQMI = 0.386102

ROOT = Path(".").resolve()
PROCESSED = ROOT / "data" / "processed"
NHD_PATH = PROCESSED / "james_river_nhd.parquet"
GRAPH_CACHE = PROCESSED / "james_river_junction_graph.pkl"

# Approximate lower James + Chesapeake Bay context (east of NHD pull terminus).
CHESAPEAKE_LON = [-77.02, -76.52, -76.08, -76.18, -76.55, -77.02]
CHESAPEAKE_LAT = [37.08, 36.88, 37.05, 37.55, 37.45, 37.08]
LOWER_JAMES_LON = [-77.538, -77.44, -77.05, -76.85, -76.25]
LOWER_JAMES_LAT = [37.561, 37.541, 37.42, 37.35, 37.12]
CITY_MARKERS = [
    ("Lynchburg", -79.14, 37.41),
    ("Richmond", -77.44, 37.54),
    ("Buchanan (NHD outlet)", -77.54, 37.58),
]


def load_flowlines():
    return gpd.read_parquet(NHD_PATH)


def geom_parts(geom):
    if geom is None or geom.is_empty:
        return []
    return [geom] if geom.geom_type == "LineString" else list(geom.geoms)


def flowline_xy(geom):
    edge_x, edge_y = [], []
    for part in geom_parts(geom):
        xs, ys = part.xy
        edge_x += list(xs) + [None]
        edge_y += list(ys) + [None]
    return edge_x, edge_y


def junction_lonlat(nhd, node):
    incoming = nhd[nhd["tonode"] == node]
    if incoming.empty:
        incoming = nhd[nhd["fromnode"] == node]
    pts = []
    for geom in incoming.geometry:
        for part in geom_parts(geom):
            pts.append(part.coords[-1])
    if not pts:
        return None, None
    return float(np.mean([p[0] for p in pts])), float(np.mean([p[1] for p in pts]))


def extended_map_ranges(bounds):
    xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax = bounds
    return (
        xmin - 0.12,
        36.72,
        -75.72,
        ymax + 0.10,
    )


def add_regional_context(fig, subplot=False, row=1, col=1):
    suffix = "" if row == 1 else str(row)
    xref = f"x{suffix}" if suffix else "x"
    yref = f"y{suffix}" if suffix else "y"
    subplot_kw = {"row": row, "col": col} if subplot else {}

    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=CHESAPEAKE_LON + [CHESAPEAKE_LON[0]],
            y=CHESAPEAKE_LAT + [CHESAPEAKE_LAT[0]],
            mode="lines",
            fill="toself",
            fillcolor="rgba(24, 95, 165, 0.18)",
            line=dict(color="rgba(24, 95, 165, 0.45)", width=1),
            name="Chesapeake Bay",
            hoverinfo="skip",
            showlegend=False,
        ),
        **subplot_kw,
    )
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=LOWER_JAMES_LON,
            y=LOWER_JAMES_LAT,
            mode="lines",
            line=dict(color="#6B8CAE", width=2, dash="dot"),
            name="Lower James (not in pull)",
            hoverinfo="skip",
            showlegend=False,
        ),
        **subplot_kw,
    )
    for name, lon, lat in CITY_MARKERS:
        fig.add_trace(
            go.Scatter(
                x=[lon],
                y=[lat],
                mode="markers+text",
                marker=dict(size=7, color="#2F3B4C", symbol="circle"),
                text=[name],
                textposition="top center",
                textfont=dict(size=10, color="#2F3B4C"),
                hoverinfo="skip",
                showlegend=False,
            ),
            **subplot_kw,
        )
    fig.add_annotation(
        x=-76.12,
        y=36.95,
        text="Chesapeake Bay",
        showarrow=False,
        font=dict(size=12, color="#185FA5"),
        xref=xref,
        yref=yref,
    )
    return fig


MAINSTEM = "James River"


def upstream_named_stream(G, node, max_hops=25):
    visited, frontier, hops = set(), [node], 0
    while frontier and hops < max_hops:
        nxt = []
        for n in frontier:
            if n in visited:
                continue
            visited.add(n)
            for pred in G.predecessors(n):
                name = str(G[pred][n].get("gnis", "")).strip()
                if name and name != MAINSTEM:
                    return name
                nxt.append(pred)
        frontier, hops = nxt, hops + 1
    return None


def edge_tributary_label(G, u, v):
    name = str(G[u][v].get("gnis", "")).strip()
    area = float(G[u][v].get("tot_area_sqmi", 0) or 0)
    if name and name != MAINSTEM:
        return name
    upstream = upstream_named_stream(G, u)
    if upstream:
        return upstream
    if area >= 1:
        return f"Headwater ({area:.0f} sq mi)"
    return "Minor tributary"


def junction_label(G, node):
    preds = list(G.predecessors(node))
    if len(preds) >= 2:
        edges = sorted(preds, key=lambda p: G[p][node].get("tot_area_sqmi", 0))
        tributary = edge_tributary_label(G, edges[0], node)
        main = str(G[edges[-1]][node].get("gnis", "")).strip() or MAINSTEM
        return f"{tributary} + {main}"
    if len(preds) == 1:
        return edge_tributary_label(G, preds[0], node)
    return f"Junction {node}"


def load_or_build_graph():
    if GRAPH_CACHE.exists():
        with open(GRAPH_CACHE, "rb") as f:
            return pickle.load(f)

    nhd = load_flowlines()
    G = nx.DiGraph()
    for _, row in nhd.iterrows():
        u, v = int(row["fromnode"]), int(row["tonode"])
        G.add_edge(
            u,
            v,
            length_mi=float(row.get("lengthkm", 0) or 0) * KM_TO_MI,
            strahler=int(row.get("streamorde", 1) or 1),
            tot_area_sqmi=float(row.get("totdasqkm", 0) or 0) * SQKM_TO_SQMI,
            comid=int(row["comid"]),
            gnis=str(row.get("gnis_name") or "").strip(),
        )

    with open(GRAPH_CACHE, "wb") as f:
        pickle.dump(G, f)
    return G


G = load_or_build_graph()
flowlines = load_flowlines()
G_u = G.to_undirected()

N = G.number_of_nodes()
M = G.number_of_edges()
n_sources = sum(1 for n in G.nodes() if G.in_degree(n) == 0)
n_sinks = sum(1 for n in G.nodes() if G.out_degree(n) == 0)
outlet = next(n for n in G.nodes() if G.out_degree(n) == 0)

total_mi = float(flowlines["lengthkm"].dropna().sum() * KM_TO_MI)
watershed_sqmi = float(flowlines["totdasqkm"].max() * SQKM_TO_SQMI)

strahler_vals = [d["strahler"] for _, _, d in G.edges(data=True) if d["strahler"] > 0]
strahler_counts = pd.Series(strahler_vals).value_counts().sort_index()

in_degrees = [G.in_degree(n) for n in G.nodes()]
out_degrees = [G.out_degree(n) for n in G.nodes()]
in_deg_counts = pd.Series(in_degrees).value_counts().sort_index()
out_deg_counts = pd.Series(out_degrees).value_counts().sort_index()
confluences = sum(1 for d in in_degrees if d >= 2)

bc = nx.betweenness_centrality(G_u, normalized=True)
bc_series = pd.Series(bc).sort_values(ascending=False)

flowlines_bc = flowlines.copy()
flowlines_bc["junction_bc"] = flowlines_bc["tonode"].map(bc_series)
flowlines_bc["length_mi"] = flowlines_bc["lengthkm"] * KM_TO_MI

path_lengths_mi = []
for src in G.nodes():
    if G.in_degree(src) == 0 and nx.has_path(G, src, outlet):
        path_lengths_mi.append(
            nx.shortest_path_length(G, src, outlet, weight="length_mi")
        )

path_arr = np.array(path_lengths_mi)
median_mi = float(np.median(path_arr)) if len(path_arr) else 0.0
p90_mi = float(np.percentile(path_arr, 90)) if len(path_arr) else 0.0
max_mi = float(np.max(path_arr)) if len(path_arr) else 0.0

map_xmin, map_ymin, map_xmax, map_ymax = extended_map_ranges(flowlines.total_bounds)
rank_colors = px.colors.sample_colorscale(
    "YlOrRd",
    [1.0 - 0.045 * i for i in range(15)],
)

bc_rank_rows = []
for rank, (node, score) in enumerate(bc_series.head(15).items(), start=1):
    preds = list(G.predecessors(node))
    drainage_sqmi = max((G[p][node]["tot_area_sqmi"] for p in preds), default=0.0)
    lon, lat = junction_lonlat(flowlines, node)
    bc_rank_rows.append(
        {
            "rank": rank,
            "node": int(node),
            "label": junction_label(G, node),
            "betweenness": score,
            "drainage_sqmi": drainage_sqmi,
            "in_degree": G.in_degree(node),
            "lon": lon,
            "lat": lat,
            "color": rank_colors[rank - 1],
        }
    )
bc_rank_df = pd.DataFrame(bc_rank_rows)

bifurcation_rows = []
orders_sorted = sorted(strahler_counts.index)
for i, order in enumerate(orders_sorted[:-1]):
    nxt = orders_sorted[i + 1]
    ratio = float(strahler_counts[order] / strahler_counts[nxt])
    bifurcation_rows.append({"Orders": f"{order} → {nxt}", "Reach ratio": f"{ratio:.2f}"})
bifurcation_df = pd.DataFrame(bifurcation_rows)
mean_bifurcation = float(np.mean([float(r["Reach ratio"]) for r in bifurcation_rows]))


def upstream_nodes(H, sink):
    return nx.ancestors(H.reverse(), sink) | {sink}


def length_connected_fraction(removed):
    H = G.copy()
    H.remove_nodes_from(removed)
    if outlet not in H:
        return 0.0
    active = upstream_nodes(H, outlet)
    connected_mi = sum(
        d["length_mi"]
        for u, v, d in G.edges(data=True)
        if u in active and v in active and u not in removed and v not in removed
    )
    return connected_mi / total_mi


def drainage_connected_fraction(removed):
    H = G.copy()
    H.remove_nodes_from(removed)
    if outlet not in H:
        return 0.0
    active = upstream_nodes(H, outlet)
    best_sqmi = 0.0
    for node in active:
        preds = [p for p in G.predecessors(node) if p in active]
        if preds:
            best_sqmi = max(
                best_sqmi,
                max(G[p][node]["tot_area_sqmi"] for p in preds),
            )
    return best_sqmi / watershed_sqmi


def robustness_curve(order, n_steps, metric, step=1):
    removed = []
    curve = [1.0]
    for i, node in enumerate(order):
        removed.append(node)
        if (i + 1) % step != 0 and (i + 1) < n_steps:
            continue
        curve.append(metric(removed))
        if (i + 1) >= n_steps:
            break
    return np.array(curve)


n_rob_steps = min(400, N - 1)
rob_step = max(1, n_rob_steps // 150)
targeted_order = sorted(G.nodes(), key=bc.get, reverse=True)
random_order = np.random.default_rng(42).permutation(list(G.nodes())).tolist()

targeted_length_S = robustness_curve(
    targeted_order, n_rob_steps, length_connected_fraction, step=rob_step
)
random_length_S = robustness_curve(
    random_order, n_rob_steps, length_connected_fraction, step=rob_step
)
targeted_drainage_S = robustness_curve(
    targeted_order, n_rob_steps, drainage_connected_fraction, step=rob_step
)
random_drainage_S = robustness_curve(
    random_order, n_rob_steps, drainage_connected_fraction, step=rob_step
)
rob_f_vals = np.linspace(0, n_rob_steps / N, len(targeted_length_S))

idx_length_50 = (
    int(np.argmax(targeted_length_S < 0.5))
    if np.any(targeted_length_S < 0.5)
    else len(targeted_length_S) - 1
)
f_length_collapse_50 = (
    float(rob_f_vals[idx_length_50]) if idx_length_50 > 0 else float(rob_f_vals[-1])
)
idx_drainage_50 = (
    int(np.argmax(targeted_drainage_S < 0.5))
    if np.any(targeted_drainage_S < 0.5)
    else len(targeted_drainage_S) - 1
)
f_drainage_collapse_50 = (
    float(rob_f_vals[idx_drainage_50]) if idx_drainage_50 > 0 else float(rob_f_vals[-1])
)
```

## Abstract

Rain that falls near Roanoke, Virginia will, if it doesn't evaporate, travel more than 200 miles through creeks, rivers, and major confluences before reaching the Chesapeake Bay. That journey follows a branching directed graph — the James River watershed — in which roughly 10,000 stream segments connect into a single drainage network. This study applies network analysis to the James River watershed as characterized by the USGS National Hydrography Dataset, treating stream junctions as nodes and stream reaches as directed edges. We describe the network's topology: its headwater sources, its Strahler order distribution, the distribution of flow-path lengths from source to mouth, and the betweenness centrality of named confluence nodes. Under random junction closure, most stream length and drainage area remain connected to the outlet; under targeted removal of high-betweenness confluences, both metrics collapse after only `{python} f"{f_drainage_collapse_50:.1%}"` of junctions are blocked. The watershed is a nearly perfect directed tree, tolerant of individual segment blockage but vulnerable at a handful of confluences where tributaries merge — locations where a dam, contamination event, or habitat barrier has outsized downstream consequence.

## Introduction

Water does not spread evenly. It collects. Every terrain feature — ridge, hill, hollow — divides the land into catchments that funnel rain toward a common low point: a stream, then a river, then a bay. The shape of that funnel is not random. It is a network: branching, hierarchical, and directional. Water moves only downstream.

Network science applied to river systems is not new — hydrologists have used graph-theoretic tools since at least Robert Horton's 1945 drainage network laws [@horton1945erosional] and Arthur Strahler's 1952 refinement of stream ordering [@strahler1952hypsometric]. What is newer is the availability of standardized, digitized watershed data at fine resolution, and the open-source tools to analyze it interactively. The USGS National Hydrography Dataset (NHDPlus) provides a topologically routed, attributed flowline network for the entire contiguous United States.

This study applies the same analytical frame used in previous Hidden Maps episodes — degree distribution, path structure, centrality, robustness — to a directed watershed graph. The subject is the **James River watershed**, which drains roughly 10,000 square miles of Virginia, from the Allegheny Highlands in the west to the Chesapeake Bay in the east.

Three questions organize this paper:

1. **Structure:** What is the degree distribution and hierarchy of the James River drainage network?
2. **Centrality:** Which confluence nodes are structural bottlenecks — the junctions whose disruption would disconnect the most upstream drainage?
3. **Directionality:** How does the directed character of a river network differ from the undirected networks studied in earlier episodes?

## Data

### Source and Acquisition

Flowline data are drawn from the **USGS National Hydrography Dataset Plus** (NHDPlus v2), accessed via the [Network Linked Data Index](https://labs.waterdata.usgs.gov/api/nldi/) (NLDI) API and the `pynhd` Python package. The outlet gauge is **USGS-02037500** (James River at Buchanan, VA), capturing the upper and middle James River drainage.

Each flowline segment carries a **fromnode** and **tonode** pair that defines how reaches connect at junctions. We build a directed graph whose nodes are hydrographic junctions and whose edges are stream reaches, annotated with Strahler order, reach length in miles, and cumulative drainage area in square miles.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-stats
#| tbl-cap: "James River watershed network statistics."

stats_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Statistic": [
        "Stream reaches (directed edges)",
        "Hydro junctions (nodes)",
        "Headwater junctions (in-degree = 0)",
        "Outlet junctions (out-degree = 0)",
        "Total stream length (miles)",
        "Watershed drainage area (sq mi)",
        "Binary-or-higher confluences (in-degree >= 2)",
        "Maximum Strahler order",
    ],
    "Value": [
        f"{M:,}",
        f"{N:,}",
        f"{n_sources:,}",
        f"{n_sinks:,}",
        f"{total_mi:,.0f}",
        f"{watershed_sqmi:,.0f}",
        f"{confluences:,}",
        f"{max(strahler_vals) if strahler_vals else 'N/A'}",
    ]
})

stats_df.style.hide(axis="index").set_properties(**{
    "text-align": "center", "font-family": "monospace"
}).set_properties(subset=["Statistic"], **{"text-align": "left"})
```

### Graph Model

The watershed is represented as a **directed acyclic graph** (DAG): nodes are hydrographic junctions; edges are stream reaches directed downstream. This is structurally different from every network analyzed in the Hidden Maps series to date. Airport routes, power transmission, street grids, and internet routing are all essentially undirected or bidirectional. Water does not back up.

## Methods

### Strahler Stream Order

Strahler ordering [@strahler1952hypsometric] assigns integer ranks to stream segments based on tributary hierarchy. Strahler order is precomputed in NHDPlus and serves here as the primary structural hierarchy metric.

### Path Length

The **flow path length** from a headwater junction to the outlet measures how far water travels through the network. We compute all shortest paths (in miles) from headwater sources to the outlet, using reach length as edge weight.

### Betweenness Centrality

Betweenness centrality measures the fraction of shortest paths between node pairs that pass through a junction. High-betweenness nodes are confluences that aggregate drainage from many upstream branches. We compute betweenness on the undirected version of the graph for comparability with prior Hidden Maps episodes, then map scores back onto stream reaches for geographic visualization.

### Robustness

Following the Hidden Maps resilience protocol from Ep. 3 (Streets), we simulate junction closure under two regimes:

- **Random failure:** junctions removed in a random order (scattered debris dams, culvert failures, low-probability blockages).
- **Targeted attack:** junctions removed in descending betweenness order (strategic barrier at the highest-centrality confluence).

Because the graph is a DAG, we track two connectedness metrics after each removal: the fraction of total stream length still upstream of the outlet, and the fraction of cumulative drainage area still routed to the outlet. Steeper decline under targeted attack quantifies the robust-yet-fragile asymmetry for directed trees.

## Results

### Geographic Map — Stream Hierarchy

```{python}
#| label: fig-map
#| fig-cap: "James River watershed flowlines colored by Strahler stream order, with geographic context east to the Chesapeake Bay. The NHD pull ends at Buchanan (upper James); the dotted line sketches the lower stem continuing to tidal water."

order_col = "streamorde"
orders = sorted(flowlines_bc[order_col].fillna(1).astype(int).unique())
palette = px.colors.sample_colorscale(
    "Blues",
    [0.2 + 0.7 * (i / max(len(orders) - 1, 1)) for i in range(len(orders))],
)

fig_map = go.Figure()
add_regional_context(fig_map)
for order, color in zip(orders, palette):
    subset = flowlines_bc[flowlines_bc[order_col].fillna(1).astype(int) == order]
    edge_x, edge_y = [], []
    for geom in subset.geometry:
        ex, ey = flowline_xy(geom)
        edge_x += ex
        edge_y += ey
    fig_map.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=edge_x, y=edge_y, mode="lines",
        line=dict(width=0.6 + 0.35 * order, color=color),
        name=f"Order {order}", hoverinfo="skip", showlegend=True,
    ))

fig_map.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(visible=False, range=[map_xmin, map_xmax]),
    yaxis=dict(visible=False, scaleanchor="x", scaleratio=1, range=[map_ymin, map_ymax]),
    template="plotly_white", height=560,
    margin=dict(l=10, r=10, t=10, b=10),
    legend=dict(title="Strahler order", orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, x=0),
)
fig_map.show()
```

The map makes the hierarchy visible (@fig-map). First-order headwater streams form a dense pale mesh across the Piedmont and Highlands; higher-order segments trace the James main stem and its major forks in darker blue. The shaded estuary and dotted lower stem show where this network ultimately delivers flow — more than 100 miles beyond the NHD pull terminus at Buchanan.

### Strahler Order Distribution

```{python}
#| label: fig-strahler
#| fig-cap: "Strahler stream order distribution across 9,925 reaches. Each successive order contains roughly three to four times fewer segments than the one below — the defining bifurcation pattern of a drainage network."

fig_str = go.Figure(go.Bar(
    x=strahler_counts.index.tolist(),
    y=strahler_counts.values.tolist(),
    marker_color="#185FA5",
    text=[f"{v:,}" for v in strahler_counts.values],
    textposition="outside",
))
fig_str.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(title="Strahler stream order", dtick=1),
    yaxis=dict(title="Number of stream reaches"),
    template="plotly_white",
    height=380,
)
fig_str.show()
```

The Strahler distribution is steeply right-skewed (@fig-strahler). The vast majority of reaches are first-order headwater streams. Maximum Strahler order is `{python} max(strahler_vals) if strahler_vals else "N/A"`.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-bifurcation
#| tbl-cap: "Strahler bifurcation ratios (reach count at order k divided by count at order k + 1). Horton's law predicts ratios near 3–5 for mature dendritic networks."

bifurcation_df.style.hide(axis="index").set_properties(**{
    "text-align": "center", "font-family": "monospace"
}).set_properties(subset=["Orders"], **{"text-align": "left"})
```

Successive ratios average `{python} f"{mean_bifurcation:.1f}"` (@tbl-bifurcation), consistent with Horton's classic bifurcation law [@horton1945erosional] — each higher order contains roughly three to four times fewer reaches than the one below.

### Junction Degree Structure

```{python}
#| label: fig-degree
#| fig-cap: "Junction in-degree and out-degree distributions. Incoming degree counts how many reaches meet at a junction; outgoing degree counts how many channels leave it. Binary confluences (in-degree = 2) dominate; the outlet is the single node with out-degree = 0."

in_labels = [str(i) for i in in_deg_counts.index]
out_labels = [str(i) for i in out_deg_counts.index]

fig_deg = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=(
        "Incoming reaches per junction (in-degree)",
        "Outgoing reaches per junction (out-degree)",
    ),
    horizontal_spacing=0.12,
)

fig_deg.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=in_labels,
    y=in_deg_counts.values.tolist(),
    marker_color="#185FA5",
    text=[f"{v:,}" for v in in_deg_counts.values],
    textposition="outside",
    name="In-degree",
), row=1, col=1)

fig_deg.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=out_labels,
    y=out_deg_counts.values.tolist(),
    marker_color="#D85A30",
    text=[f"{v:,}" for v in out_deg_counts.values],
    textposition="outside",
    name="Out-degree",
), row=1, col=2)

fig_deg.update_xaxes(title_text="Degree", row=1, col=1)
fig_deg.update_xaxes(title_text="Degree", row=1, col=2)
fig_deg.update_yaxes(title_text="Number of junctions", row=1, col=1)
fig_deg.update_yaxes(title_text="Number of junctions", row=1, col=2)
fig_deg.update_layout(template="plotly_white", height=420, showlegend=False)
fig_deg.show()
```

The degree structure is tree-like but not uniform (@fig-degree). **`{python} f"{in_deg_counts.get(2, 0):,}"`** junctions receive exactly two incoming reaches — classic binary confluences where two tributaries merge. **`{python} f"{in_deg_counts.get(0, 0):,}"`** junctions are headwater sources with no incoming flow. Out-degree is almost always 1: water leaves a junction through a single downstream channel. The lone outlet (out-degree = 0) is the watershed mouth.

Unlike scale-free networks (airports, internet), there are no hubs with dozens of direct connections. Hierarchy emerges through sequential merging downstream.

### Flow Path Lengths

```{python}
#| label: fig-paths
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative distribution of flow-path length from headwater junctions to the outlet. Most headwaters are close to the main stem; the long upper tail reflects western Highland tributaries traveling the full valley length."

sorted_paths = np.sort(path_arr)
cdf_y = np.arange(1, len(sorted_paths) + 1) / len(sorted_paths)

fig_paths = go.Figure()
fig_paths.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=sorted_paths,
    y=cdf_y,
    mode="lines",
    line=dict(color="#185FA5", width=2.5),
    name="Empirical CDF",
))
for val, label, color in [
    (median_mi, f"Median {median_mi:.0f} mi", "#D85A30"),
    (p90_mi, f"90th pct {p90_mi:.0f} mi", "#3B6D11"),
    (max_mi, f"Max {max_mi:.0f} mi", "#7A5195"),
]:
    fig_paths.add_vline(
        x=val, line=dict(color=color, dash="dot", width=1.8),
        annotation_text=f"  {label}", annotation_font_size=11,
    )

fig_paths.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(title="Flow-path length from headwater to outlet (miles)"),
    yaxis=dict(title="Fraction of headwater junctions", tickformat=".0%"),
    template="plotly_white",
    height=400,
)
fig_paths.show()
```

The median flow path is `{python} f"{median_mi:.0f}"` miles; 90% of headwater junctions lie within `{python} f"{p90_mi:.0f}"` miles of the outlet (@fig-paths). The maximum path is `{python} f"{max_mi:.0f}"` miles — the Jackson and Cowpasture headwaters crossing the full length of the valley before reaching tidal water.

Roughly **`{python} f"{(path_arr <= 50).mean():.0%}"`** of headwaters drain to the outlet in under 50 miles. The long tail is where network structure becomes visceral: a raindrop near Warm Springs faces a `{python} f"{max_mi:.0f}"`-mile journey.

### Confluence Explorer

```{python}
#| label: tbl-confluences
#| tbl-cap: "Top 15 confluences by betweenness centrality. Bar length in @fig-betweenness uses upstream drainage area because raw betweenness values compress on a tree."

confluence_tbl = bc_rank_df.copy()
confluence_tbl["betweenness"] = confluence_tbl["betweenness"].map(lambda v: f"{v:.4f}")
confluence_tbl["drainage_sqmi"] = confluence_tbl["drainage_sqmi"].map(lambda v: f"{v:,.0f}")
confluence_tbl = confluence_tbl[
    ["rank", "label", "betweenness", "drainage_sqmi", "in_degree"]
]
confluence_tbl.columns = [
    "Rank",
    "Confluence",
    "Betweenness",
    "Upstream drainage (sq mi)",
    "In-degree",
]
confluence_tbl.style.hide(axis="index").set_table_styles([
    {"selector": "th", "props": [
        ("font-size", "13px"), ("padding", "8px 12px"), ("text-align", "center"),
    ]},
    {"selector": "td", "props": [
        ("font-size", "13px"), ("padding", "8px 12px"), ("line-height", "1.4"),
    ]},
]).set_properties(**{
    "text-align": "center", "font-family": "inherit",
}).set_properties(subset=["Confluence"], **{"text-align": "left"})
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-betweenness
#| fig-cap: "Interactive confluence explorer: click any ranked bar to zoom the map and highlight that junction. Bar length shows upstream drainage area; color matches rank. The map extends east to the Chesapeake Bay for geographic context."

bc_vals = flowlines_bc["junction_bc"].fillna(0)
bc_palette = px.colors.sample_colorscale("YlOrRd", np.linspace(0.12, 1.0, 8).tolist())

fig_exp = make_subplots(
    rows=2,
    cols=1,
    row_heights=[0.58, 0.42],
    vertical_spacing=0.06,
    subplot_titles=(
        "Watershed map — click a bar below to locate the confluence",
        "Top 15 confluences — upstream drainage area",
    ),
)

add_regional_context(fig_exp, subplot=True, row=1, col=1)

for lo, hi, color in zip(
    np.linspace(0, bc_vals.max(), 8)[:-1],
    np.linspace(0, bc_vals.max(), 8)[1:],
    bc_palette,
):
    subset = flowlines_bc[(bc_vals >= lo) & (bc_vals < hi)]
    if subset.empty:
        continue
    edge_x, edge_y = [], []
    for geom in subset.geometry:
        ex, ey = flowline_xy(geom)
        edge_x += ex
        edge_y += ey
    fig_exp.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=edge_x,
            y=edge_y,
            mode="lines",
            line=dict(width=1.0, color=color),
            opacity=0.55,
            hoverinfo="skip",
            showlegend=False,
            name="flow-bc",
        ),
        row=1,
        col=1,
    )

marker_sizes = np.clip(
    12 + 0.004 * bc_rank_df["drainage_sqmi"].to_numpy(),
    12,
    34,
).tolist()
marker_colors = bc_rank_df["color"].tolist()

fig_exp.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=bc_rank_df["lon"],
        y=bc_rank_df["lat"],
        mode="markers+text",
        marker=dict(
            size=marker_sizes,
            color=marker_colors,
            line=dict(width=1.5, color="#ffffff"),
            symbol="circle",
        ),
        text=[str(r) for r in bc_rank_df["rank"]],
        textfont=dict(size=10, color="#ffffff"),
        textposition="middle center",
        customdata=bc_rank_df[["label", "drainage_sqmi", "betweenness"]].values,
        hovertemplate=(
            "<b>#%{text}: %{customdata[0]}</b><br>"
            "%{customdata[1]:,.0f} sq mi upstream<br>"
            "Betweenness %{customdata[2]:.3f}<extra></extra>"
        ),
        name="confluence-markers",
    ),
    row=1,
    col=1,
)

bar_rank_labels = [f"#{r}" for r in bc_rank_df["rank"]]

fig_exp.add_trace(
    go.Bar(
        x=bc_rank_df["drainage_sqmi"],
        y=bar_rank_labels,
        orientation="h",
        marker=dict(
            color=marker_colors,
            line=dict(color="#ffffff", width=1.2),
        ),
        text=bc_rank_df["label"].tolist(),
        textposition="inside",
        insidetextanchor="start",
        textfont=dict(size=11, color="#ffffff"),
        customdata=bc_rank_df[["betweenness", "drainage_sqmi"]].values,
        hovertemplate=(
            "<b>%{text}</b><br>"
            "%{customdata[1]:,.0f} sq mi upstream<br>"
            "Betweenness %{customdata[0]:.3f}<extra>Click to locate on map</extra>"
        ),
        name="confluence-bars",
    ),
    row=2,
    col=1,
)

fig_exp.update_xaxes(visible=False, range=[map_xmin, map_xmax], row=1, col=1)
fig_exp.update_yaxes(
    visible=False,
    scaleanchor="x",
    scaleratio=1,
    range=[map_ymin, map_ymax],
    row=1,
    col=1,
)
fig_exp.update_xaxes(
    title_text="Upstream drainage area (sq mi)",
    range=[0, bc_rank_df["drainage_sqmi"].max() * 1.08],
    row=2, col=1,
)
fig_exp.update_yaxes(
    autorange="reversed",
    tickfont=dict(size=13),
    automargin=True,
    title_text="Rank",
    row=2, col=1,
)
link_config = {
    "defaultSizes": marker_sizes,
    "defaultColors": marker_colors,
    "lons": bc_rank_df["lon"].tolist(),
    "lats": bc_rank_df["lat"].tolist(),
}
fig_exp.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=980,
    margin=dict(l=60, r=30, t=50, b=30),
    barmode="overlay",
    showlegend=False,
    meta=dict(confluence_link=json.dumps(link_config)),
)
fig_exp.show()
```

The ranked panel in @fig-betweenness uses rank numbers on the left axis and prints full confluence names inside each bar; @tbl-confluences repeats the same rows in a readable static table. Bar length uses upstream drainage area because raw betweenness values compress on a tree (0.50–0.56 for ranks 6–15).

Betweenness concentrates along the James main stem and its major tributary confluences (@fig-betweenness). These are not abstract node IDs — they are real places where named rivers meet. **Click any bar** to zoom the map and highlight that junction; marker rank and color match the bar.

The highest-betweenness confluence — **`{python} bc_rank_df.iloc[0]['label']`** — carries roughly **`{python} f"{bc_rank_df.iloc[0]['drainage_sqmi']:,.0f}"` square miles** of upstream drainage. A barrier or contamination event there does not affect one creek; it affects everything above it on the network.

### Network Robustness

```{python}
#| label: fig-robustness
#| fig-cap: "Watershed robustness under random junction closure and targeted attack on high-betweenness confluences. Two metrics: stream length and cumulative drainage area still connected to the outlet."

fig_rob = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=(
        "Stream length still connected",
        "Drainage area still routed to outlet",
    ),
    horizontal_spacing=0.10,
)

for y, color, name in [
    (random_length_S, "#185FA5", "Random failure"),
    (targeted_length_S, "#D85A30", "Targeted attack"),
]:
    fig_rob.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=rob_f_vals, y=y, mode="lines", name=name,
        line=dict(color=color, width=2.5),
        legendgroup=name, showlegend=True,
    ), row=1, col=1)

for y, color, name in [
    (random_drainage_S, "#185FA5", "Random failure"),
    (targeted_drainage_S, "#D85A30", "Targeted attack"),
]:
    fig_rob.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=rob_f_vals, y=y, mode="lines", name=name,
        line=dict(color=color, width=2.5),
        legendgroup=name, showlegend=False,
    ), row=1, col=2)

if idx_drainage_50 > 0:
    fig_rob.add_vline(
        x=f_drainage_collapse_50,
        line=dict(color="#888780", dash="dot", width=1.5),
        annotation_text=f"  50% drainage loss<br>  at {f_drainage_collapse_50:.1%} removal",
        annotation_font_size=10,
        row=1, col=2,
    )

fig_rob.update_xaxes(title_text="Fraction of junctions removed", tickformat=".0%", row=1, col=1)
fig_rob.update_xaxes(title_text="Fraction of junctions removed", tickformat=".0%", row=1, col=2)
fig_rob.update_yaxes(title_text="Connected stream length (fraction)", range=[0, 1.05], row=1, col=1)
fig_rob.update_yaxes(title_text="Routed drainage area (fraction)", range=[0, 1.05], row=1, col=2)
fig_rob.update_layout(template="plotly_white", height=460, legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98))
fig_rob.show()
```

The divergence between random and targeted curves (@fig-robustness) is the central resilience result. Random junction closure — analogous to scattered culvert failures or debris dams — barely disconnects the network: most stream length and drainage area remain routed downstream. Targeted removal of high-betweenness confluences collapses both metrics rapidly; drainage area falls below 50% after removing only **`{python} f"{f_drainage_collapse_50:.1%}"`** of junctions.

This is the same robust-yet-fragile pattern documented in Ep. 3 (Streets), but the mechanism is purely topological: there are no alternate downstream paths in a DAG. Blocking **`{python} bc_rank_df.iloc[0]['label']`** severs every upstream tributary that depended on that merge.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-comparison
#| tbl-cap: "Structural comparison across Hidden Maps episodes."

comparison_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Property": [
        "Directed?",
        "Typical node degree",
        "Alternate paths?",
        "Hub structure",
        "Primary chokepoint metric",
        "Random failure tolerance",
    ],
    "Airports (Ep. 1)": [
        "No", "Heavy-tailed", "Often yes", "Scale-free hubs", "Betweenness", "Moderate",
    ],
    "Streets (Ep. 3)": [
        "No", "Low (~3)", "Often yes", "Bridge bottlenecks", "Betweenness", "High",
    ],
    "Watershed (Ep. 5)": [
        "Yes (DAG)", "in ~2, out ~1", "Never", "Main-stem confluences", "Betweenness", "Very high",
    ],
})
comparison_df.style.hide(axis="index").set_properties(**{
    "text-align": "center", "font-family": "monospace", "font-size": "11pt",
}).set_properties(subset=["Property"], **{"text-align": "left"})
```

---

## Discussion

**River networks are DAGs, and that matters.** Every prior episode in this series analyzed undirected or bidirectional networks. Rivers cannot reroute. If a confluence is blocked, upstream tributaries have no alternate path.

**The Strahler hierarchy compresses complexity.** First-order reaches collectively account for most stream length, yet management attention historically focused on navigable main stems. The network view suggests headwater protection yields the largest aggregate gain per dollar.

**Comparison to prior episodes.** @tbl-comparison summarizes the pattern: airports and internet AS graphs are undirected, hub-dominated, and reroutable; watersheds are directed trees with binary merges and zero alternate paths. Random failure tolerance is higher than any prior episode; targeted confluence attack is more consequential.

**Data scope.** The NHD pull terminates at Buchanan (USGS-02037500), covering the upper and middle James (~`{python} f"{watershed_sqmi:,.0f}"` sq mi). The lower James to Richmond and tidal Chesapeake Bay are sketched for geographic context but not yet in the graph — extending the pull to a tidal outlet would close the gap between this analysis and the full ~10,000 sq mi James–Chesapeake system.

---

## Conclusion

The James River watershed is a directed tree of approximately `{python} f"{total_mi:,.0f}"` miles of stream length draining `{python} f"{watershed_sqmi:,.0f}"` square miles of Virginia. As a network it is structurally simple — binary merges, single outlets — and structurally unforgiving: no alternate paths, and named high-betweenness confluences are true chokepoints. Random reach failures are absorbable; the right confluence failure is catastrophic.

Every drop goes somewhere. The map shows where.

---

*Hidden Maps is a series on the networks embedded in everyday infrastructure. Previous episodes: [Ep. 0 — Primer](https://altustd.github.io/network-analysis-intro/), [Ep. 1 — Flights](https://altustd.github.io/airport-network-analysis/airport-network-analysis.html), [Ep. 2 — The Grid](https://altustd.github.io/us-power-grid-network/us-power-grid-network.html), [Ep. 3 — Streets](https://altustd.github.io/road-network-resilience/road-network-resilience.html), [Ep. 4 — Internet](https://altustd.github.io/internet-as-topology/internet-as-topology.html).*

## References
